scenario: Meridian Bank runs a loan origination process on Camunda 8.8 with 40 process variables per instance and 6500 jobs per day. A specific job handler only reads 2 of those variables. The platform team must minimize broker load and network bandwidth on job activation while keeping the rest of the variables untouched on the instance.
question: Which job worker configuration does the best-practices guide recommend to minimize data transferred on job activation?

options.text: Keep the default activation behaviour so every variable visible in the scope is fetched, then ignore the unused ones inside the worker handler code at runtime
Set the FetchVariables parameter on the worker to the specific variable names the handler needs, so only those variables are read and transferred from the broker
Disable variable activation entirely on the worker and read each required variable through a separate REST call against the Operate variables API at job start
Activate every variable in the scope but compress the payload on the broker side using gzip so the network bandwidth stays low even for large instances

a.text: Incorrect. By default the job payload contains all variables in scope; the guide explicitly recommends configuring FetchVariables rather than relying on the default.

b.text: Correct. The guide recommends using the FetchVariables parameter and only fetching the variables the handler needs to keep transferred data minimal and improve performance.

c.text: Incorrect. The recommended mechanism is the FetchVariables parameter on the job worker, not an out-of-band call to the Operate variables API.

d.text: Incorrect. The guide focuses on minimizing what data is read on activation via FetchVariables, not on compressing the full payload of every variable in scope.

explanation: Camunda 8.8 job workers can declare the FetchVariables parameter to receive only the variables a handler actually needs on activation, saving broker resources and network bandwidth compared to fetching the full set of in-scope variables.
